title: CHAPTER 13 —
UNDERSTANDING AND USING DATABASES

The spreadsheet approach — everything in one table, one row per event — makes sense until it breaks. It breaks at the mo

Chapter 13 — Understanding and Using Dat — You have a table with 5,000 appointment rows. Each row has: clinic ID, clinic name, clinic address, clinic phone, patien

beats.text: The spreadsheet approach , everything in one table, one row per event , makes sense until it breaks. It breaks at the moment you need to update something that appears in many rows. You have a table with 5,000 appointment rows. Each row has: clinic ID, clinic name, clinic address, clinic phone, patient ID, patient first name, patient last name, patient birth date, appointment date, appointment type, provider name, status. The clinic "Berkeley West" appears in 347 rows.

segments.label: 01 / 11 · HOOK

title: THE RELATIONAL
MODEL.
segments.body_html:
C001 — Berkeley West
C002 — Oakland Central
patient_id — first_name
P001 — Alice

beats.text: A relational database stores data in multiple separate tables, each representing one kind of thing. The tables are linked by keys , identifiers that let a row in one table point to a row in another. For the clinic problem, the structure looks like this:

beats.text: A query is a request. You state what you want, and the database finds it. The language for doing this is SQL , Structured Query Language , and it has been the standard since IBM invented it in the 1970s. The basic structure of a query is:

segments.label: 03 / 11 · HOW YOU ASK A DATABASE QUESTIO

title: HOW STRUCTURE PREVENTS ERRORS
BEFORE THEY HAPPEN.
segments.body_html: First scenario: hidden lists.→Second scenario: data that belongs to something else.→Third scenario: stored calculations.

beats.text: The normalization problem deserves its own examination, because it is the kind of thing that feels like pedantic database theory until you have been burned by it once. First scenario: hidden lists. You have a customers table, and someone adds a column called phone_numbers that stores multiple phone numbers as a comma-separated string: "510-555-0001, 510-555-0002." The column looks sensible until you try to query it. "Find all customers whose phone number starts with 510" , the database cannot parse a list hidden inside a text field.

segments.label: 04 / 11 · HOW STRUCTURE PREVENTS ERRORS

title: VERIFICATION:
WHY QUERIES NEED CHECKING
segments.body_html:
A SQL query is precise in a way a spreadsheet filter is not. But precision can be precisely wrong.

Verification: Why Queries Need Checking — The most common error is a condition that looks correct but excludes rows you intended to include. The clinic spreadshee

beats.text: A SQL query is precise in a way a spreadsheet filter is not. But precision can be precisely wrong. The most common error is a condition that looks correct but excludes rows you intended to include. The clinic spreadsheet problem at the start of this chapter , the 18 missing appointments because appointment_status was blank rather than "cancelled" , would behave the same way in a SQL query. WHERE appointment_status = 'cancelled' does not match NULL.

segments.label: 05 / 11 · VERIFICATION: WHY QUERIES NEED

title: WHAT STRUCTURE
BUYS YOU.
segments.body_html:
The relational model asks something of you at the beginning: think carefully about what tables you need, what each one c

What Structure Buys You — What it buys you is permanence. The Berkeley West problem — where updating one clinic's address means touching 347 rows

beats.text: The relational model asks something of you at the beginning: think carefully about what tables you need, what each one contains, and how they relate. That structural work is overhead before the first row of data is entered. It takes time you might not feel you have. What it buys you is permanence. The Berkeley West problem , where updating one clinic's address means touching 347 rows , does not exist in a well-designed relational database.

segments.label: 06 / 11 · WHAT STRUCTURE BUYS YOU

title: EXERCISES
segments.body_html: 13.1→13.2→13.3→13.4→13.5

beats.text: 13.1 (The one-thing-many-places problem.) A bookstore tracks sales in a spreadsheet. Each row is one sale. Columns include: book title, author name, author email, customer name, customer email, sale date, quantity, price. The author Margaret Atwood has sold 412 books this year. How many times is her email address stored? What happens when she changes her email? Name the specific error this creates and describe the fix using the language of this chapter. 13.2 (Primary keys and foreign keys.) You are designing a database for a school.

segments.label: 07 / 11 · EXERCISES

Add the constraint"Answer using one specific kind of query optimization (join ordering, index selection, or predicate pushdown) and what t

beats.text: The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Patricia Selinger was working on the IBM System R query optimizer , the 1979 algorithm that lets a SQL query against millions of rows return in milliseconds, the engine inside every relational database you've ever touched decades before most people had heard of databases. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

segments.label: 08 / 11 · AI WAYBACK MACHINE
